coredata.dc:title: Energy band and interface engineering of ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs photoanode with enhanced optical absorption and charge transfer for efficient photoelectrochemical water oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Zemin
Zhang, Weiye
Qin, Dongmei
Han, Sheng
Dong, Zhenbiao
coredata.dc:description: Suitable energy band alignment and interface design of high-performance Ti-based photoanode is an effective way to address the easy compounding of photo-generated carriers, inferior light harvesting and poor oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics. Hence then, the present work constructed a TiO2 nanotube arrays/g-C3N4/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs) composite system with enhanced optical absorption and charge transfer for efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation. As photosensitive components and light capture units, both g-C3N4 and NCQDs significantly increased light absorption of the composite photoanode. Continuous type-Ⅱ heterojunction formed in the composite photoanode through quantitative analysis of energy band positions. Based on the positive interfacial reaction, PEC water oxidation ability and bulk-phase charge separation efficiency were enhanced. Microstructure analysis combined with PEC measurements indicated that g-C3N4 and NCQDs enhanced near-UV-vis absorption, and OER kinetics of TNAs was boosted with reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the photoanode/electrolyte solid-liquid interface. This well-matched energy level arrangement effectively inhibited the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers, while electrons and holes transfer to lower and higher energy levels across solid-solid interfaces with a formation of efficient and directional charge transfer channel. Photocurrent densities of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs under full light and visible-light-driven reached 4.7 mA cm−2 and 0.52 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which were 7.8 and 167 times of pristine system, respectively. This work concerned energy band and interface engineering of ternary Ti-based nanotube photoanodes, may provide fresh insights into creating multicomponent composite photoanodes for high-performance PEC water oxidation.

simple-head.ce:title: Energy band and interface engineering of ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs photoanode with enhanced optical absorption and charge transfer for efficient photoelectrochemical water oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Suitable energy band alignment and interface design of high-performance Ti-based photoanode is an effective way to address the easy compounding of photo-generated carriers, inferior light harvesting and poor oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics. Hence then, the present work constructed a TiO2 nanotube arrays/g-C3N4/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs) composite system with enhanced optical absorption and charge transfer for efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation. As photosensitive components and light capture units, both g-C3N4 and NCQDs significantly increased light absorption of the composite photoanode. Continuous type-Ⅱ heterojunction formed in the composite photoanode through quantitative analysis of energy band positions. Based on the positive interfacial reaction, PEC water oxidation ability and bulk-phase charge separation efficiency were enhanced. Microstructure analysis combined with PEC measurements indicated that g-C3N4 and NCQDs enhanced near-UV-vis absorption, and OER kinetics of TNAs was boosted with reduced charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the photoanode/electrolyte solid-liquid interface. This well-matched energy level arrangement effectively inhibited the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers, while electrons and holes transfer to lower and higher energy levels across solid-solid interfaces with a formation of efficient and directional charge transfer channel. Photocurrent densities of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs under full light and visible-light-driven reached 4.7 mA cm−2 and 0.52 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which were 7.8 and 167 times of pristine system, respectively. This work concerned energy band and interface engineering of ternary Ti-based nanotube photoanodes, may provide fresh insights into creating multicomponent composite photoanodes for high-performance PEC water oxidation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting based on semiconductor has been extensively investigated in recent years to produce photo-generated electrons-holes pair under light illumination [1,2]. Under a certain bias pressure, photo-generated electrons and holes were transmitted to photocathode and photoanode respectively. And then the electrons and holes react with water to form oxygen and hydrogen by oxidation and reduction, respectively [3,4]. In the context of global fossil energy depletion and serious pollution, PEC hydrolysis avoids the pollution of fuel and energy products caused by platform construction process and has a wide range of clean and sustainable development prospects [5]. As the earliest and most widely developed photocatalytic material, TiO2 is an n-type semiconductor with low-cost, green and non-polluting characteristics. In addition, its stable performance and various structure brought about extensive modification researches in the field of photocatalysts/photoelectrochemical [6–8]. In particular, one-dimensional nanostructured such as nanotubes, nanofibers and nanorods with faster axial charge transport paths, greater specific surface area and wider light response range provide more reactive sites have attracted great interest [2,9–13]. When photo-generated electrons are generated, the narrower band gap means that it is readier for electrons to be excited from VB to CB, resulting in higher electron mobility and energy conversion efficiency [5,14,15]. Compared with common photocatalytic semiconductors, the photo-generated charge carriers of TiO2 exhibit a high propensity of recombination owing to its relatively large band gap energy (∼3.2 eV). However, forming heterojunctions with other semiconductors like type-Ⅱ energy band alignment could hinder this situation [6,16–18]. When the interface of two intercalated semiconductors is in good contact, the photo-generated electrons from both sides will converge at semiconductor CB at the lower energy band position, while holes will converge at VB of the other semiconductor. This process effectively enhances the exploitation proportion of electrons and holes involved in water oxidation and hydrogen generation in catalysis, instead of recombination of photo-generated carriers [1,15,17]. Currently, some Ti-based type-II heterojunctions have been reported. However, there is still deficiency of low charge separation and injection efficiency in the bulk-phase leading to low water oxidation at the solid-liquid interface. Hence, how to build a more efficient interface charge transfer has become a problem that needs to be solved in a pluralistic system [1,7,15,19].

Conjugated polymers are usually characterized by high stability, ease of electron transfer and wide visible light response [20–23]. In addition to foregoing features, as a typical π-conjugative polymeric semiconductor, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) possesses a narrow band gap of 2.6–2.7 eV [7,20,24]. In recent years, coupling TiO2 with g-C3N4 have been developed and studied in many fields such as photodegradation [25], water oxidation [24,26] and CO2 reduction [27] etc. Due to its suitable energy band position, the formation of type-Ⅱ heterojunctions at the solid-solid interface facilitated the directional transport of photo-generated charges to a certain extent [28]. Some 2D g-C3N4 nanosheets coupling with TiO2 nanotubes formed a 3D structure to enhance utilizing sunlight [13,29]. In addition, coupling some smaller size g-C3N4 with 1D TiO2 nanotubes with confinement effect has improved PEC performance due to increased reactive sites [7,30,31]. Up to now, construction of g-C3N4 and TiO2 type-Ⅱ heterojunction still has some defects of low charge mobility and limited light absorption.

As a new-type optoelectronic material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have excellent prospects in the field of PEC water oxidation because of their low cost, environmental friendliness, high electron mobility, strong chemical inertness and excellent optical properties [32–35]. CQDs could be acted as electron transport ligands due to the suitable energy band positions, this hindered holes and electrons recombination and directing holes toward CQDs [36–38]. Optically, decorating CQDs could cause a large red-shift on the absorption band edge of TiO2, as well as a great near-UV-visible light absorption was obviously enhanced [37,39]. CQDs possess a photoluminescence range spanning almost entire spectrum, and the photoluminescence properties of specific excitation wavelengths were suppressed when compounded with TiO2, this promoted both photoconversion efficiency and separation of photo-generated carriers [39–41]. As emerging CQDs materials, N-doped CQDs were used in some photocatalysis and other fields because of their broadened photoresponse range and enhanced electron transport capability [42,43]. Zhao et al. [44] modified S-doped g-C3N4 with N-doped CQDs for photocatalysis degradation, tubular g-C3N4 provided a large number of loading sites for CQDs while broadening photoresponse range and thus enhancing light absorption. Wang et al. [45] induced the transformation of bulk CQDs/g-C3N4 into porous ultrathin 2D nanosheets with high specific surface area through heat treatment. The introduced CQDs tuned intrinsic electronic states of g-C3N4 and improved charge carriers transfer capability, photocatalytic activity has been significantly improved. Preeyanghaa et al. [46] promoted the generation of nitrogen vacancies by doping boron with g-C3N4, which accelerated charge separation/transfer, and the binding of CQDs broadened the light capture ability through photon upconversion.

Based on above prerequisites and analysis, ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs photoanode concerned energy band and interface engineering was successfully prepared through a simple two-step physical impregnation for high-performance water oxidation. Optical characterizations showed that the composite photoanode had a well-matched energy band structure and enhanced light absorption. As photosensitive components and light capture units, both g-C3N4 and NCQDs significantly increased visible light absorption of the composite photoanode. The continuous type-Ⅱ heterostructures had established an efficient and directional charge transfer channel, which effectively improve transport rate of photo-generated carriers. As a result, oxygen evolution reaction kinetics of the composite photoanode had been proven to be greatly enhanced. Photocurrent density of the ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQD composite photoanode under full light and visible-light-driven was 7.8 and 167 times that of pristine system, respectively. Corresponding solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency was 3.58 %, which was 7.46 times higher than pristine system. This work concerned photosensitive units, energy band and interface engineering of ternary Ti-based nanotubes photoanode, may provide fresh insights into exploring the synergistic enhancement mechanism for achieving higher PEC performance.

All chemical reagents were analytical grade without additional purification and were bought from commercial sources. Ti-foil (20 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm, 99.5%) was self-prepared by vacuum arc melting technology. TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs) was fabricated using anodic oxidation. To remove oxide layer and oil impurities from the surface, Ti foil was first polished until smooth, then sonicated in anhydrous ethanol and deionized water and dried, respectively. Glycol solution of ammonium fluoride was used as electrolyte, treated Ti foil and graphite plate was used as anode and cathode, respectively. And then it was kept in an acrylic electrolytic cell, applied 40 V to the cathode and anode and hold for 1 hour. Finally, after calcining at air atmosphere, highly ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs) photoanodes were obtained.

Bulk g-C3N4 involved calcining melamine at a temperature of 550 °C for 4 h, conducted in an air environment. Bulk g-C3N4 was transformed into a powdered form through grinding. Subsequently, a g-C3N4 suspension with a consistency of 10 mg mL−1 was prepared by subjecting it to sonication in a solution consisting of ethanol and water in 3:1. N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) was conducted using a previously established one-step hydrothermal technique [47]. 20 mM citric acid with 1.5 mL ethylenediamine as C and N sources were mixed and stirred well with 50 mL DI, transferred to 100 mL PTFE liner in a stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h. Then the solution was dialyzed through MW100 D dialysis bag for 24 hours to obtain NCQDs aqueous dispersion. TNAs were immersed in the prepared g-C3N4 suspension and kept at 60 °C for 5, 15 and 25 h, and they were subjected to vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h. This prepared composite was named TNAs/g-C3N4. Prepared TNAs/g-C3N4 were immersed in NCQDs solution for 5, 15 and 25 h, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h under vacuum to obtain TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs photoanodes with different loadings. The TNAs after x hours of g-C3N4 soaking with y hours of NCQDs were named TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs-x-y. The explicit preparation process details were depicted in Scheme 1 .

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Ultima IV, Japan). Morphology and elemental components of photoanodes were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS GeminiSEM 300, Germany) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray microanalysis (EDS). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) examination was performed using K-Alpha + XPS equipment (Thermo Scientific Inc., USA). Photoluminescence (PL) characteristic was assessed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK) with a 400 nm excitation wavelength. The investigation involved the analysis of ultraviolet–visible absorbance spectra (UV–vis/Absorbance) using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 900, USA). Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA).

Photoelectrochemical properties were measured using a three-electrode system linked to an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, CH Instruments Corp., Shanghai). The performance measurements were conducted in a solution containing 0.5 M Na2SO4 and 0.5 M Na2SO3. The Ag/AgCl, Pt sheet and photoanode electrode were employed as the reference electrode, counter electrode and working electrode, respectively. Moreover, measurements that necessitated the extra lighting were carried out under light conditions of 100 mW cm−2 intensity, provided by a 350 W lamp (Shanghai Lansheng Electronics Co., Ltd.). Photocurrent density under visible light was tested through a 420 nm filter. The area of the platinum plate electrode was 4 cm2. A 200 mL beaker with a volume of 150 mL electrolyte was used as the electrolysis cell. The testing area of the photoanode was 1 cm2, and the unit of photocurrent density was evaluated by mA cm−2. The reversible hydrogen electrode potential (E
RHE ) could be achieved using the Nernst formula [37]:

where pH indicates pH value of the electrolyte. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured at 5 mV amplitude perturbation and with frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. Linear scanning voltammetric (LSV) tests were performed over −0.5 V–1.5 V vs. RHE. I-t tests were performed at 1.23 V vs. RHE. Moreover, Mott-Schottky (MS) measurements were conducted with −0.4 V–1.6 V vs. RHE.

Microscopic morphology and microstructure of the different photoanodes were observed by SEM (Figs. 1 a–d). As seen in the top view image, nanotubes of the TNAs sample grew closely arranged with a clear and highly ordered tubular shape (Fig. 1a) [25,46], surface of the photoanode was neat and standardized without obvious impurities. For TNAs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 1b), g-C3N4 was deposited at the TNAs surface along the wall shape, and overall loading was relatively uniform, in addition, morphology of the TNAs was not changed by loading as seen through the few tube orifices without loads. The deposition of g-C3N4 for TNAs exhibited an overall layer of irregular nanoparticle agglomeration on the surface, which might be attributed to the physical deposition inhomogeneity, thus obtaining a larger specific surface area and enhancing the loading sites and reactive sites of NCQDs [18,25,48]. As seen in Fig. 1c, NCQDs on the tube was not obvious at the surface, which was probably due to the high dispersion of NCQDs and their small particle size [37,49]. Some agglomerated NCQDs were seen in orifice, which might be attributed to tubular structure [50]. In the top view of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, the load characteristics of g-C3N4 and NCQDs appeared simultaneously (Fig. 1d). The load situation of g-C3N4 did not undergo any significant changes after being soaked in NCQDs. However, agglomerates of NCQDs appeared inside the tube, signifying the successful loading of g-C3N4 and NCQDs on.

nanotubes simultaneously. Cross-sectional image of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs was shown in Fig. 1d, the length of grown nanotube arrays reached 1.5 μm, while the deposition process did not change their morphology and structure. Figs. 1e–h displayed EDS mappings of Ti, O, C and N elements at the cross-section, which corresponded to Fig. 1d of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, the C and N elements were uniformly distributed along tubes, which proved that g-C3N4 and N-doped CQDs were equally uniformly distributed in tube except for the stacking at tube top [48,50]. EDS patterns of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, TNAs, TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/NCQDs were demonstrated in Fig. 1i and Figs. S2a–c, respectively. Elements mass fractions and atomic contents of N and C for TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs were higher than both TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/NCQDs. In addition, the interfacial contact and agglomeration among TiO2 nanotubes, g-C3N4 and NCQDs were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Figs. 1j–k, the g-C3N4 was uniformly loaded on the TNAs tubes and orifices in the form of thin film coverings with particles, which was consistent with the findings in the SEM. Moreover, the interfacial contact between TNAs, g-C3N4 and NCQDs was more clear and showed distinct lattice stripes in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1l). TNAs exhibited lattice fringe spacing with d = 0.35 nm and d = 0.24, which corresponds to the (101) and (001) crystal planes of anatase TiO2, respectively. The g-C3N4 at the interfacial contact showed the (002) crystal plane corresponding to the graphite phase, while the NCQDs in the overall image showed lattice spacing of d = 0.19 in many places, corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of graphite.

Through XRD patterns, crystalline structures of photoanodes and g-C3N4 were characterized (Fig. 2 a). All diffractions at 25.3°, 37.9°, 38.5°, 48.1°, 54.1°, 55.2° and 62.8° were well-matched with (101), (004), (112), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116) and (215) crystal planes of anatase without obvious impurity peaks [7,10,41,48]. The diffraction peak of g-C3N4 at 27.4° coincided with (002) crystal plane, and corresponding diffraction peaks were observed at 27.4° in TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, indicating that g-C3N4 was successfully loaded onto TNAs [26,51]. Diffraction peaks of the TNAs/NCQDs photoanode were almost the same as TNAs without new diffraction peaks, which might be attributed to the poor crystallinity of NCQDs [34,41]. According to the positions and intensities of the anatase phase diffraction peaks, the crystal of TNAs was not interfered by g-C3N4 and NCQDs, this might be attributed in the low loading amount.

Surface chemical structure and interaction nature among g-C3N4, NCQDs and TNAs were probed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 3454 cm−1 of TNAs could be attributed to adsorbed water and hydroxyl groups (-OH) stretching vibration, in addition to the involvement of free amino groups (N-Hx), which were reflected in the corresponding positions of g-C3N4, NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs [7,44,52,53]. Characteristic peaks in the g-C3N4 spectra located at 885 cm−1 corresponded to skeletal vibrations and characteristic respiration patterns of triazine groups [7,54]. A series of strong bands in a range of 1080 cm−1 to 1539 cm−1 were typical C–N heterocycles stretching, where the characteristic peaks at 1245 cm−1, 1325 cm−1, 1395 cm−1 corresponded to aromatic ring stretching vibration with C]C, and the same stretching vibration was observed at the corresponding positions on TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs [43,44]. The characteristic peaks at 1219 cm−1, 1395 cm−1 and 1555 cm−1 corresponded to C–O, C–N and C–C bonds stretching vibrations, except for the hydroxyl groups at 3454 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 positions, which were reflected in TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs [42,55]. This was probably due to the high dispersion and low loading of NCQDs, which reduced the tensile vibration strength of bonds.

Elemental composition and surface chemical state of photoanodes were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Survey XPS spectrum of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs and high-resolution Ti 2p, O 1s, C 1s and N 1s XPS spectra were shown in Figs. 2c–g. Calibration of all peaks was performed using the standard C 1s (284.8 eV) [40]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the spectra analysis revealed the presence of Ti, O, C, and N elements on TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, TNAs/NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4, while N was not detected in TNAs. In addition, the C and N peaks of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs were significantly stronger than those of TNAs/NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4, and the C peaks of both composites photoanodes were stronger than those of TNAs. This was consistent with EDS results, and conspicuous impurity peaks were not found overall. As seen in the high-resolution Ti 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d), Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 spin orbitals of the Ti4+ state were positioned at 464.39 eV and 458.69 eV with an interval of 5.7 eV, and this was consistent with binding energy and spin energy separation of the Ti4+ state in TiO2 [18,52,54]. This result indicated that TiO2 was produced by anodic oxidation of Ti substrate [51,56]. By combining the peak intensities comparison and binding energy of Ti 2p in different photoanodes (Fig. S3a), NCQDs and g-C3N4 weakened the Ti 2p intensity without shifting.

binding energy. This may be attributed to the change in electron cloud density caused by the partial substitution of Ti–N bonds for Ti–O bonds [54], and the lowest peak intensity exhibited by the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs samples was also consistent with this result. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum was displayed in Fig. 2e, the peaks at binding energy 531.21 eV and 529.81 eV correspond to the surface –OH group and the Ti–O band at the interface between g-C3N4 and TNAs, respectively [30,51]. As in the case of Ti 2p, composite photoanode O 1s also did not undergo binding energy shift, but the complex chemical environment of g-C3N4, NCQDs and TiO2 with multifunctional groups underwent electron cloud density change and peak intensity was thus reduced [11,53,54]. Notably, as Ti–O bond peak strength decreased, –OH group instead got an enhanced peak intensity, this might be attributed to g-C3N4 and NCQDs promoted chemisorption or dissociation of water molecules [57]. As seen in Fig. 2f, high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum showed that C 1s were divided into three anti-convolution peaks: N–C]C at 288.33 eV, C–O at 286.23 eV and C–C (C]C) at 284.8 eV [51,58]. As shown in Fig. S3c, the intensity of C–C (C]C) and N–C]N peaks of composite photoanode was significantly higher compared to pristine TNAs, while a stronger C–O bond appeared due to the good coupling of C atoms with TNAs at the interface [3,41,50,51]. As seen in Fig. 2g, high-resolution N 1s spectrum was convolved into three peaks (401.25 eV, 399.68 eV and 398.82 eV) which corresponded to N-Hx, N-(C)3 and C–N]C in the triazine unit, respectively [51,52]. As shown in Fig. S3d, N-(C)3 groups indicated the possible presence of melamine polymerization, while N–H or N–H2 groups proved that melamine pyrolysis to g-C3N4 was incomplete condensation and also explained the TNAs/NCQDs bonding [54]. Fig. 2h showed the VB positions of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, TNAs/g-C3N4, TNAs/NCQDs and pristine TNAs. After fitting, the loading of g-C3N4 and NCQDs moved the VB position towards low binding energy to varying degrees, which showed an upward shift in the energy band structure. This phenomenon could be explained by the VB positions of g-C3N4 and NCQDs in Fig. 2i. When semiconductors with different VB positions combined, high VB and low VB semiconductors usually interact with each other [59].

Optical properties of TNAs, TNAs/g-C3N4, TNAs/NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs were studied by Ultraviolet–visible absorbance spectra (UV–vis/Absorbance). As seen in the inset in Fig. 3 a, the pristine TNAs light absorption at visible light range was relatively weak with light absorption band edge pointing to 425 nm. In contrast, TNAs/g-C3N4 exhibited enhanced light absorption as a whole. Moreover, introduced g-C3N4 produced a certain light absorption band edge red-shift pointed to 466 nm. Meanwhile, the Tauc's plots (Fig. 3a) were conveniently obtained by the Kubelka-Munk (KM) conversion [18]:
(2)
(
α h v
)
2
= A
(
h v − E g
)
where ν, h and α denote photon frequency, Planck's constant and absorption coefficient, respectively. A denotes constant and Eg is band gap [57]. As shown in Fig. 3a, TNAs/g-C3N4 band gap was narrowed compared to pristine TNAs after the Kubelka-Munk conversion. These results were well-matched with UV–vis results of g-C3N4 (Fig. S5a), while the calculated Eg was displayed in Fig. S5b. A schematic diagram of energy band structure between TNAs and g-C3N4 could be constructed by the Eg and VB of g-C3N4. A typical type-Ⅱ heterojunction between TNAs and g-C3N4 was formed at the interface, while a certain energy band bending was generated due to the electrons convergence. This facilitated forming efficient interfacial charge transport channels, which could guide photo-generated carrier vector transport and enhance interfacial water oxidation.

capacity of the photoanode [34,49,60]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3a, NCQDs further enhanced the overall light absorption, especially in a certain visible light range (420 nm–500 nm). At the same time, photo absorption band edges were substantially red-shifted to 568 nm, which proved that optical absorption capacity of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs was substantially enhanced and band gap was further reduced. As shown in Fig. 3a, the band gaps of TNAs, TNAs/g-C3N4, TNAs/NCQDs, TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs were 3.21 eV, 3.09 eV, 3.05 eV and 2.8 eV, respectively. In addition, UV–vis absorbance spectra of NCQDs and corresponding Eg were shown in Fig. S5, and VB of NCQDs was shown in Fig. 2i. This indicated that there was a reasonable energy band position of NCQDs in the composite photoanode, which was consistent with the characteristics of continuous type-Ⅱ heterojunction in the ternary structure, and thus an ideal charge transfer path was constructed while light absorption was greatly enhanced [61]. In addition, the light harvesting efficiency (LHE) of the samples were shown in Fig. S7d. It could be observed that the LHE after loading g-C3N4 and NCQDs increased by about 4% in UV range, while the improvement in visible light range was relatively significant. Loads of g-C3N4 and NCQDs enhanced the light absorption capacity of TNAs.

Fig. 3b showed photoluminescence analysis of TNAs, TNAs/g-C3N4, TNAs/NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs to reveal the effect of NCQDs and g-C3N4 for photoinduced holes and electrons separation [10,51]. After g-C3N4 deposition, TNAs/g-C3N4 had a lower PL luminous peak than TNAs, this indicated the heterojunction structure inhibited electrons and holes complexation [41]. Both TNAs/NCQDs and the ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs had extremely low PL luminous peaks, which suggested that NCQDs inhibited the electrons-holes pairs recombination of TNAs efficiently.

PEC properties of the photoanodes were evaluated by LSV curves, I-t curves, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Mott-Schottky measurements. As shown in Fig. 4 a, pristine TNAs exhibited an inferior transient photocurrent, which was 0.59 mA cm−2 at 1.23V vs. RHE. By optimizing g-C3N4 loading on TNAs, photocurrent of the TNAs/g-C3N4 could reach 1.33 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, and with a negative shift of onset potential occurred. TNAs combined with g-C3N4 successfully enhanced photoelectrochemical performance, while positively influencing the onset of water oxidation reaction. This phenomenon of negative shift of onset potential was also found in TNAs/NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, the optimized photocurrent density of TNAs/NCQDs reached 3.48 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. Corresponding optimized photocurrent density of g-C3N4 and NCQDs compounded with TNAs were shown in Figs. S6a–d, respectively. The performance enhancement was not significant when the immersion time of g-C3N4 dispersion reached 5 h, while the highest performance enhancement of TNAs was obtained after the deposition time reached 15 h. However, continuing to extend the immersion time up to 25 h, the performance of TNAs/gC3N4 decreased significantly. This result suggested that low loading could not completely contact with the nanotubes, while large loading would lead to excessive accumulation of deposition and reduced the number of reactive sites, thus decreasing the performance. The optimization process for NCQDs also reflected this. Performance tests of deposition volume optimization were shown in Figs. S6e–f, TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs exhibited a transient.

photocurrent density of 4.7 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. This optimization process for the ternary system showed that 15 h immersion was the optimal reaction time for the monomer loading and the ternary system. It could be found from the comparison of NCQDs and g-C3N4 coupling alone with TNAs that photoelectrochemical performance enhancement of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs over pristine TNAs was stronger than simple superposition of g-C3N4 and NCQDs. This phenomenon might be attributed to the fact that g-C3N4 played a role in expansion of deposit-able reactive sites for small-sized NCQDs and positive synergistic effect in water oxidation process due to energy band-matched continuous type-II heterogeneous interface. Solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiencies (η) of the photoanodes were determined using Eq. (3) [62,63]:
(3)
η = J
(
1.23 − V R H E
)
/ J l i g h t
where J is photocurrent density at measured bias, V
RHE is reversible hydrogen electrode and J
light denotes the 100 mW cm−2 irradiance intensity. As seen in Fig. 4b, STH efficiency of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs reached 3.58%, which was 7.46, 4.47 and 1.6 times than pristine TNAs, TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/NCQDs, respectively. LSV curves of the samples loaded with NCQDs produced spikes at the moment of illumination, which might be caused by aggregation of transient photo-generated charges [64]. Chopper transient I-t curves for full light and visible light (>420 nm) were evaluated at 1.23 V vs. RHE. Tested performance could be seen in Fig. 4c, TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs exhibited competent photocurrent stability and photoresponse characteristics under switched light/dark condition [62]. Photocurrent curve was smooth overall, and photocurrent density results were consistent with LSV results. As seen in Fig. 4d, composite photoanode exhibited significant photocurrent density enhancement, which reached 0.52 mA cm−2 in visible light domain by coupling g-C3N4 and NCQDs. This was 167 times stronger than pristine TNAs and matched with UV–vis absorbance spectra. Long-cycle performance of the samples was shown in Fig. 4e. After 18,000 s stability testing TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs maintained about 90% of its initial photocurrent density, which was basically consistent with the stability of pristine TNAs (91%). This result indicated that the combination of NCQDs and g-C3N4 did not affect the stability of the substrate.

To reveal the inner interaction for improved photoelectrochemical performance at the solid-liquid interface, bulk-phase separation efficiency (η
sep ) of the photoanodes was analyzed. And it could be obtained from Eq. (4) [37,41,62]:
(4)
η s e p = J N a 2 S O 3 / N a 2 S O 4 / J a b s
where J
Na2SO3/Na2SO4 is photocurrent density after a hole sacrificial agent (Na2SO3) to bring interfacial injection efficiency to 100% for better individual observation of interfacial separation efficiency. J
abs is the theoretical photocurrent determined by optical absorption band edge in UV–vis. As seen in Fig. 5 a, the calculated bulk-phase charge separation efficiency of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs reaching 47%, which were 2.1 times higher than pristine TNAs. Matched energy bands and three-dimensional microstructure created an efficient vectorial charge transfer path for the photoanode, promoting efficient hole transport and consumption at the solid-liquid interface [14,65]. As seen from Tafel slopes of the different photoanodes (Fig. 5b), NCQDs significantly lowered overpotential, while TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs had the lowest overpotential (269 mV dec−1). The effect of single g-C3N4 loading was not significant, but combining g-C3N4 with NCQDs in the ternary system had a certain positive and synergies effect. This demonstrated that the efficient charge transport channel built by the continuous type-Ⅱ heterostructures enhanced the separation and transport of photo-generated carriers and inhibited recombination, thus significantly reduced the Tafel slope and enhanced the water oxidation kinetics [58,65,66].

Solid-liquid interface charge transfer properties of the photoelectrode/electrolyte were also investigated by EIS Nyquist plot, Bode plot and phase angle-frequency plot. As shown in Fig. 6 a, TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs showed the most minimal electron transfer resistance radius. A smaller radius typically indicates a lower level of interfacial electron transfer resistance, indicating that the constructed vector interfacial channels successfully enhanced conductivity and promote charge transfer [51,67]. An equivalent circuit was proposed to interpret Nyquist plot (Fig. 6a attached), in which the external resistance included experimental platform and electrolyte was represented by Rs [68]. It was noteworthy that the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs heterostructure showed the lowest Rct, which indicated higher interfacial charge transfer kinetics due to a reasonable energy band structure, while TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/NCQDs were relatively high [69]. Also, CPE was a constant phase element for the non-ideal nature of Helmholtz layer capacitance. These results showed that introducing g-C3N4 and NCQDs extended the advantage of low impedance, photo-generated charges could be more easily transferred through the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface [57,60]. The fitted electrochemical parameters values were shown in Table S1. After loading with g-C3N4 and NCQDs, the values of Rs and Rct decreased. The value of CPE-T decreased after loading but matched the experimental value, while the value of CPE-P always approached 1, indicated that CPE was a pure capacitor [41,70]. Furthermore, Bode plot (Fig. 6b) with Nyquist under illumination. Fig. S7a showed the same regularity as Nyquist in dark state, photo-generated carriers were similarly enhanced by the lower transfer resistance of constructed interfacial channel. Lifetime of the transferred electrons was evaluated by Bode phase-frequency diagram. As shown in Fig. 6c, TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs in the high frequencies exhibited high oxygen evolution reaction kinetics, while carrier lifetimes (τ) were estimated by Eq. (5) [46,48,71]:
(5)
τ = 1 / 2 π
f
where f is relaxation frequency obtained from the maximum value in Bode frequency range. By comparing the different τ calculated from the corresponding peaks positions in Fig. 6c, TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs possessed the largest carrier lifetime. This indicated that fast interfacial channel suppresses electrons-holes recombination and possessed a strong charge transport capacity, thus enhanced interfacial oxygen evolution reaction kinetics, which was consistent with Nyquist plots [46,72]. To study the semiconductor and electronic properties of photoanodes, Mott-Schottky plots were measured in a range of −0.4 V–1.6 V vs. RHE (Fig. 6d). Corresponding flat-band potential (VF) and carrier density could be estimated by the following Eqs. (6) and (7) [57]:

Flat-band potential of TNAs was calculated to be 0.52 V vs. RHE, while it was negatively shifted to −0.09 V vs. RHE after coupling g-C3N4 and NCQDs. The negative shift indicated an increase in energy band bending degree, which in turn enhanced driving force of interfacial charge transport and effectively enhanced oxygen evolution reaction kinetics. Moreover, flat-band potentials of TNAs/NCQDs and TNAs/g-C3N4 photoanodes were also significantly negatively shifted, which matched the energy band structure [17,41,57]. The n-type semiconductors characteristics were found on all photoanodes, demonstrating that coupling g-C3N4 with NCQDs did not change the conductive properties. Besides, the carrier concentrations of different photoanodes were calculated by Eq. (7) (Fig. S7e). TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs were 1.76, 1.50 and 1.22 times of TNAs, TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/NCQDs, respectively. Higher carrier concentration represented higher conductivity and carrier transport capability [57]. LSV curves for electrocatalytic water oxidation could also be used to some extent to assess the OER activity [57,73]. To further confirm water oxidation capacity, the different photoanodes overpotentials were determined in dark at 1.8–3 V vs. RHE. Selection of the tangent location to onset potential was further determined by fitting and deriving the LSV curves in the dark state (Fig. S7c). As seen in Fig. S7b, TNAs/g-C3N4 and TNAs/NCQDs had some negative overpotential shift relative to TNAs. Moreover, overpotential of the TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs was negatively shifted to 2.09 V, which was 0.49 V difference from TNAs. The lower overpotential meant higher water oxidation kinetics, which reduced energy barrier under applied solar-light field and promoted the interfacial OER [37,57,59].

Eg and VB positions of TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs, g-C3N4 and NCQDs were obtained from above XPS with UV–vis analysis. After energy band structure construction, the proposed charge separation-transport mechanism of composite system for PEC water splitting was shown in Fig. 7 . TiO2 nanotube array films were grown on the surface by anodic oxidation using Ti foil as substrate. g-C3N4 and NCQDs were sequentially loaded on the nanotube surface, forming a ternary composite structure. Results of Eg revealed that the band gaps of TNAs, NCQDs and g-C3N4 were 3.21 eV, 2.74 eV and 2.27 eV, respectively. Moreover, A clear tendency of Eg narrowing and energy band shifting up were observed in ternary structure. According to the direction and characteristics of photo-generated electrons transport in the photoanode, narrow outer and wide inner energy band structure of the composite system makes it easier for outer light capture unit to obtain enhanced light capture efficiency [17]. At the same time, the inner structure and outer energy band arrangement could be obtained by combining with VB, and TNAs, g-C3N4 and NCQDs formed a type-Ⅱ heterojunction. After photoexcitation, photo-generated electrons-holes pairs of g-C3N4 and TNAs got separated, the photo-generated electrons migrate to their respective CBs, while electrons transfer to the lower CB of TNAs, while holes transfer to VB of g-C3N4, which was located at a higher energy level. HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) and LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) positions of NCQDs were derived from the calculation of VB and Eg. Similar to mechanism between g-C3N4 and TNAs, electrons-holes transfer path of g-C3N4 coupling NCQDs also follows type-Ⅱ heterojunction [37,41,57]. The difference was ascribed to that NCQDs affect transfer path of photo-generated electrons through an anti-Stokes process. π orbital electrons in NCQDs jumped to π* orbitals by photon excitation, and a portion were transferred to g-C3N4 or TNAs for hydrogen production reaction by external bias and type-Ⅱ heterojunction, and part of photo-generated electrons fell back to σ orbitals to produce anti-Stokes shift, accompanied by PL luminescence [67]. When photoanode was immersed in the electrolyte, rapid electron transfer occurred at the photoanode/electrolyte solid-liquid interface and equilibrium was quickly reached [12]. Along with formation of heterojunctions, the energy band bending between TNAs and g-C3N4 was simultaneously at the interface, while the flat-band potential was displaced [63]. As a result, the constructed continuous type-Ⅱ heterojunction in ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs effectively enhanced charge separation-transfer between solid-solid/solid-liquid interface and significantly improved the PEC performance for efficient solar-driven water splitting.

In summary, ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQDs composite photoanode with energy band and interface engineering was successfully prepared for efficient PEC water oxidation. As photosensitive components and light capture units, both g-C3N4 and NCQDs significantly increased visible light absorption of the composite photoanode. Optical characterization indicated that near-UV-vis absorption of the composite photoanode was significantly enhanced, and the band edge significantly red-shifted from 425 nm to 568 nm. The formed continuous type-II heterostructure among TNAs, g-C3N4 and NCQDs established efficient and directional charge transfer channels, this effectively improved the separation-transport of photo-generated carriers. In addition, the oxygen evolution reaction kinetics of composite photoanode have been analyzed and proven to be greatly boosted. Photocurrent density of the ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQD composite photoanode under full light and visible-light-driven reached 4.7 mA cm−2 and 0.52 mA cm−2, which were 7.8 and 167 times that of the pristine nanotubes, respectively. Moreover, solar-to-hydrogen (STH) efficiency of ternary TNAs/g-C3N4/NCQD reached 3.58% which was 7.46 times higher than pristine nanotubes. All the results above demonstrated that appropriate band matching and interface regulation on the composite photoanode have a positive effect on improving oxygen evolution reaction kinetics. We hoped that this work concerned energy band and interface engineering may provide a valuable theoretical basis for rational designing high-performance photoelectrodes for PEC water splitting.
